text: This is a thirty-two-year-old female who presents with lower GI bleed and malnutrition secondary to ulcerative colitis, which is refractory to medical management. She is taken to the operating room for a laparoscopic total colectomy.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic total colectomy, progressing from the medial to lateral aspect of the colon.
The surgeon begins dissection of the left colon by identifying the ligament of Treitz. The inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is located lateral to the ligament, skeletonized, and divided.

The surgeon retracts the left mesocolon anteriorly, identifying the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and the ureter crossing the pelvic brim. The dissection continues to the level of the left colic branch of the IMA. The IMA pedicle is identified, and retroperitoneal structures are swept down before the IMA is divided.
The surgeon dissects the lateral attachments of the rectosigmoid colon and divides the colon at the rectosigmoid junction using an endogia stapler.
The surgeon dissects the mesocolon from the underlying retroperitoneal structures, and the splenic flexure is completely mobilized.
The surgeon identifies, skeletonizes, and divides the left middle colic vessels during the laparoscopic total colectomy.

The surgeon begins the dissection of the right colon by identifying the ileocolic pedicle. The duodenum is visible, and the retroperitoneal structures are swept down. The ileocolic pedicle is retracted and dissected, followed by the division of the ileocolic vessels.
The surgeon identifies the right middle colic vessels, sweeps down the retroperitoneal structures, and skeletonizes and divides the vessels.

The surgeon takes down the hepatic flexure, followed by the dissection of the lateral attachments of the right colon from the abdominal wall. The distal ileum is then divided using surgical instruments.

A dilator is inserted into the rectum, and electrocautery is used to open the rectal stump. Ring forceps are introduced transanally into the abdomen to grasp the specimen, which is then extracted through the anus under direct visualization.
The surgeon grasps the rectal stump and uses a surgical stapler to transect it, removing the remnant rectum from the abdominal cavity.
Pathology revealed, ulcerative colitis with no dysplasia.
